text: HE et al.: AUTOMATICALLY IDENTIFYING CVE AFFECTED VERSIONS WITH PATCHES AND DEVELOPER LOGS 915

In the end, there are 12 partial matching versions of CVE,

which makes it impossible to determine whether there are any vulnerabilities in this study. There are 74 corresponding versions, accounting for nearly 3.6% of 337 CVEs. Among the remaining 325 CVEs, if some matching codes are directly identiﬁed as vulnerabilities by our tool, through log veriﬁcation, it is found that there are 19 CVEs with false positives and 4 CVEs with false negatives, corresponding to 172 versions and 39 versions respectively. Therefore, assuming that all 74 unconﬁrmed versions have corresponding CVE vulnerabilities, the total number of false negatives is 113 (74 plus 39) versions, and the total number of true positives is 4669 versions, so the lower limit of recall rate of this tool is 97.6%. At the same time, the number of false positives is 172 versions, and the corresponding number of true positives is 4610 versions, so the correct rate of this tool is 96.4%. In the end, this scheme corrected 19+4 = 23 CVE vulnerabilities that were incorrectly identiﬁed by the tool through log veriﬁcation.
D. Incorrect Results of Known Affected Software Versions of NVD
In this section, we will introduce the difference between the

result of our tool and the Known Affected Software Versions of the NVD. We use the boundary versions ( V upto , V i.j.U , V from ) in the version tree for each vulnerability, which we have veriﬁed previously. It is worth noting that in this section we introduce FP/FN for the NVD information, not FP/FN of our tool.
In Figs. 10 and 11 , for fairness, we sample 273 versions at
intervals of 5 versions for mapping. The x-axis represents the CVE-IDs from 2011/2003 to 2020 and the y-axis represents the
Fig. 13. The error rate of last vulnerable version set of NVD.

version numbers. Due to the large amount of data, we hide the scale of the x-axis. In Fig. 10 , we can observe that in many cases the maximum committed versions in the changelogs, the upto versions (i.e., the last vulnerable version) of NVD, and the V upto reported by our method are different. That is, in many cases neither the upto versions reported by NVD nor the changelog based maximum committed versions are correct. Take CVE- 2013-1826 as an example (see Fig. 12 ), for the branch of major version V 3 . 4 , its V i.j.U should be minor version V 3 . 4 . 13 , which is our veriﬁed version, but the NVD reported version is V 3 . 4 . 122 . Therefore, it is regarded as a false negative case. In the second example, for CVE-2017-15868, its V upto veriﬁed by our tool is V 3 . 18 . 63 , but NVD reported V 3 . 18 . 66 as its upto version; therefore, this is regarded as a false positive case. In another example, for CVE-2017-15951, its V upto veriﬁed by our tool is V 4 . 13 . 9 , but the NVD upto version is V 4 . 12 . 14 , which is regarded as a false negative case.
Fig. 11 shows the report on the from versions (i.e., the ﬁrst

vulnerable version) of each CVE in the NVD, and it can be seen that most of the from versions it reported are version V 1 . 2 . 0 . Throughourmethod,weﬁndthatinmostcases, V from isgreater than version 1.2.0, which indicates that there are a lot of false positives on the from versions of the NVD. For example, in our vulnerable version set, V from of CVE-2015-7550 should be version V 2 . 6 . 10 , but the NVD report is V 1 . 2 . 0 , so this from version is regarded as a false positive. Finally, under our dataset, we found 11,827 versions incorrectly reported in NVD. Our study also shows that some versions missed records in changelogs.
